concept: each element doubles the number of paths
cue: At every element the function calls itself twice, once for each choice, until it runs out of elements.
syntax: a recursive call that branches in two
note: For each element you either take it or skip it: two choices per element, so three elements give 2 * 2 * 2 = 8 paths. This is the shape of every subset problem.

concept: build all subsets by adding the first element to each subset of the rest
cue: Work out the subsets of the tail first, then make a second copy with the first element added in front.
note: The subsets of [1, 2] are the subsets of [2] plus those same subsets with 1 added. The empty subset is always the base case.

concept: pop after recursing restores the path to what it was
cue: Every push is undone by a pop after the recursive call returns, so track the length that gets recorded at each entry.
note: Because each push is paired with a pop, the path returns to empty by the end. Choose, explore, then un-choose is the pattern behind every backtracking search.

concept: the search records the empty set first, then grows each branch
cue: Follow the calls in order: record the path on entry, then for each element push it, recurse, and pop before trying the next.
note: Each call first records where it is, then dives deeper. The empty set comes out first because it is recorded before any element is chosen.

concept: permutations pick from the unused elements each step
cue: The first slot has three choices, the next has the two left, the last has one. Multiply them.
syntax: skip an index that is already used
note: A full arrangement uses every element once, so the counts are 3 * 2 * 1 = 6. Order matters here, which is what separates permutations from subsets.

concept: a partial string is legal only while closes never pass opens
cue: Add a close only when there are more opens than closes so far, and add an open only while opens are below n.
syntax: open < n and close < open guards
note: The two guards prune anything that could not become balanced, so the search only ever builds valid strings. For n = 1 the single answer is ().

cue: Record a copy of the current path at every call, then for each later element add it, recurse from the next index, and remove it.
syntax: record path.slice() at every node, loop i from start
prompt: Write `subsets(nums)` that returns every subset of `nums` (the power set), each as an array. `nums` has distinct values. The subsets may come back in any order. For `[]` return `[[]]`.
note: Choose an element, explore deeper from the next index, then un-choose by popping. There are 2^n subsets, so the work is O(n * 2^n).

cue: When the path holds every element, record it; otherwise loop over the elements not yet used, adding and later removing each.
syntax: track which indexes are used; stop when path is full
prompt: Write `permute(nums)` that returns every ordering of `nums`, each as an array. `nums` has distinct values. The orderings may come back in any order, but each inner array must be a real ordering. For `[]` return `[[]]`.
note: Choose an unused element, explore, then un-choose by freeing it again. There are n! orderings, so the work is O(n * n!).

cue: Record the path once it holds k numbers; otherwise loop from the current start up to n, adding a number and recursing from the next one.
syntax: stop when path length reaches k; recurse from i + 1
prompt: Write `combine(n, k)` that returns every combination of `k` numbers chosen from `1..n`, each as an array. The combinations may come back in any order. Numbers within a combination must not repeat.
note: Choose a number, explore from the next value so nothing repeats and order does not vary, then un-choose. This is the subsets pattern with a size limit, O(k * C(n, k)).

concept: reuse comes from recursing at the same index
cue: Subtract the chosen value from what is left; record when it hits zero and abandon a branch once it goes below zero.
note: Staying at the same index lets a number repeat, so 2 + 2 + 2 and 3 + 3 both reach 6. Recursing from the current index (not the next) is what allows reuse.

concept: combinations that sum to a target, reuse allowed
cue: Subtract the chosen number from what remains; record the path at zero, give up below zero, and recurse from the same index so a number can repeat.
syntax: recurse from i (not i + 1) to allow reuse
prompt: Write `combinationSum(candidates, target)` that returns every unique combination of `candidates` that adds up to `target`, each as an array. Each candidate may be used any number of times. `candidates` has distinct positive values. The combinations may come back in any order. When none exist, return `[]`.
note: Choose a candidate, explore from the same index so it can repeat, then un-choose. Recursing from the current index (not the next) both allows reuse and stops the same set appearing in a different order.

concept: all valid parenthesis strings of n pairs
cue: Grow the string one character at a time: add an open while opens are below n, add a close only while closes trail opens, and record it at length 2n.
syntax: guard with open < n and close < open
prompt: Write `generateParentheses(n)` that returns every string of `n` pairs of parentheses that is well-formed, in any order. For `n = 0` return `['']`.
note: The two guards are the choose step: they only ever build a string that can still become balanced, so no invalid string is generated and none needs discarding. The count is the nth Catalan number.
